title: Reconstruction
description: Test your skills on how policy during Reconstruction affected the South!

assessment_items.question_content:
>"It is idle to say that a citizen shall have the right to life, yet to deny him the right to labor, whereby alone he can live. It is a mockery to say that a citizen may have a right to live, and yet deny him the right to make a contract to secure the privilege and the rewards of labor. It is worse than mockery to say that men may be clothed by the national authority with the character of citizens, yet may be stripped by State authority of the means by which citizens may exist. . . .

> “It is barbarous, inhuman, infamous, to turn over four million liberated slaves, always loyal to the government, to the fury of their rebel masters, who deny them the benefit of all laws for the protection of their civil rights.”

>-Source: Representative William Lawrence, *Congressional Globe,* 1866

**The key concern that the excerpt was designed to address was the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: violations of African American rights at the state level of government
demand for African American voting rights in federal elections
lack of military presence in the southern states to protect African Americans
continued enslavement of African Americans after the Civil War

assessment_items.question_content:
>"It is idle to say that a citizen shall have the right to life, yet to deny him the right to labor, whereby alone he can live. It is a mockery to say that a citizen may have a right to live, and yet deny him the right to make a contract to secure the privilege and the rewards of labor. It is worse than mockery to say that men may be clothed by the national authority with the character of citizens, yet may be stripped by State authority of the means by which citizens may exist. . . .

> “It is barbarous, inhuman, infamous, to turn over four million liberated slaves, always loyal to the government, to the fury of their rebel masters, who deny them the benefit of all laws for the protection of their civil rights.”

>-Source: Representative William Lawrence, *Congressional Globe,* 1866

**The excerpt most strongly suggests that in 1866 which of the following was correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There was a lack of clarity about African American citizenship status after the ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment.
The Emancipation Proclamation defined a path to citizenship for free African American men.
The US Army  promised the extension of citizenship to any man who served in the military against the Confederacy.
The US government kept black men in service rather than grant them total personal freedom.

assessment_items.question_content:
>"It is idle to say that a citizen shall have the right to life, yet to deny him the right to labor, whereby alone he can live. It is a mockery to say that a citizen may have a right to live, and yet deny him the right to make a contract to secure the privilege and the rewards of labor. It is worse than mockery to say that men may be clothed by the national authority with the character of citizens, yet may be stripped by State authority of the means by which citizens may exist. . . .

> “It is barbarous, inhuman, infamous, to turn over four million liberated slaves, always loyal to the government, to the fury of their rebel masters, who deny them the benefit of all laws for the protection of their civil rights.”

>-Source: Representative William Lawrence, *Congressional Globe,* 1866

**The primary source author most likely wrote his account for which of the following reasons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to encourage the federal government to grant birthright citizenship
to propose a migration plan to relocate African Americans to the North
to recruit US Army service members to protect African Americans in the South
to advocate for a job training program to give African Americans new skills

assessment_items.question_content:
>"It is idle to say that a citizen shall have the right to life, yet to deny him the right to labor, whereby alone he can live. It is a mockery to say that a citizen may have a right to live, and yet deny him the right to make a contract to secure the privilege and the rewards of labor. It is worse than mockery to say that men may be clothed by the national authority with the character of citizens, yet may be stripped by State authority of the means by which citizens may exist. . . .

> “It is barbarous, inhuman, infamous, to turn over four million liberated slaves, always loyal to the government, to the fury of their rebel masters, who deny them the benefit of all laws for the protection of their civil rights.”

>-Source: Representative William Lawrence, *Congressional Globe,* 1866

**Which of the following developments best represents a logical extension of the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the passage of the first Civil Rights Act
the order of the Emancipation Proclamation
the establishment of the Freedmen’s Bureau
the ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
> “If we put negro regiments there and give them the bayonets, why can’t we give votes? They have joined in putting down the Rebellion; and now to place them at the mercy of those they have helped us to subdue — to deny them all political rights — to give them freedom but leave them entirely subject to laws framed by Rebel masters — is an act of injustice against which humanity revolts.”

>-Source: Senator John Sherman of Ohio, *National Anti-Slavery Standard,* 1865

**The author’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the mid-nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: debates about the extension of suffrage beyond white men
disagreements over the re-admittance of southern states
disputes over the end of slavery in the border states
tensions between Republicans and Democrats after the Civil War

assessment_items.question_content:
> “If we put negro regiments there and give them the bayonets, why can’t we give votes? They have joined in putting down the Rebellion; and now to place them at the mercy of those they have helped us to subdue — to deny them all political rights — to give them freedom but leave them entirely subject to laws framed by Rebel masters — is an act of injustice against which humanity revolts.”

>-Source: Senator John Sherman of Ohio, *National Anti-Slavery Standard,* 1865

**The ideas in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the passage of the Reconstruction Acts
the establishment of the Ku Klux Klan
the creation of Jim Crow laws across the South
the occupation of the South by the US Army

assessment_items.question_content:
> “If we put negro regiments there and give them the bayonets, why can’t we give votes? They have joined in putting down the Rebellion; and now to place them at the mercy of those they have helped us to subdue — to deny them all political rights — to give them freedom but leave them entirely subject to laws framed by Rebel masters — is an act of injustice against which humanity revolts.”

>-Source: Senator John Sherman of Ohio, *National Anti-Slavery Standard,* 1865

**Which of the following federal actions most directly supported the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the ratification of the Fifteenth Amendment
the passage of the Homestead Act
the introduction of Black Codes
the enactment of the Civil Rights Act of 1875

assessment_items.question_content:
> “‘Manhood Suffrage’ creates an antagonism everywhere between educated, refined women and the lower orders of men especially in the South, where the slaves of yesterday are the law-makers of to-day. . . Just as the democratic cry of a ‘white man’s government,’ created the antagonism between the Irishman and the negro, which culminated in the New York riots of ‘63, so the republican cry of ‘manhood suffrage’ creates an antagonism between black men and all women, that will culminate in fearful outrages on womanhood, especially in the Southern States.” 

>-Source: Elizabeth Cady Stanton, “Women and Black Men,” *Revolution,* 1869

**The excerpt best serves as evidence of which of the following trends during Reconstruction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the tension over the Fifteenth Amendment within the women’s rights movement
the intensity of debates over the extension of suffrage to Irish and German immigrants
the rise in a women’s rights movement with the stated goal of extending suffrage to women
the creation of anti-black groups in the South that aimed to suppress African American votes

assessment_items.question_content:
> “‘Manhood Suffrage’ creates an antagonism everywhere between educated, refined women and the lower orders of men especially in the South, where the slaves of yesterday are the law-makers of to-day. . . Just as the democratic cry of a ‘white man’s government,’ created the antagonism between the Irishman and the negro, which culminated in the New York riots of ‘63, so the republican cry of ‘manhood suffrage’ creates an antagonism between black men and all women, that will culminate in fearful outrages on womanhood, especially in the Southern States.” 

>-Source: Elizabeth Cady Stanton, “Women and Black Men,” *Revolution,* 1869

**Stanton’s reference to ‘Manhood Suffrage’ most directly reflects which of the following policy changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the extension of voting power to all men born or naturalized in the United States
the elimination of property qualifications for voting in federal elections
the ratification of an amendment that banned the institution of slavery
the creation of an organization to educate  newly-freed African Americans in the South

assessment_items.question_content:
> “‘Manhood Suffrage’ creates an antagonism everywhere between educated, refined women and the lower orders of men especially in the South, where the slaves of yesterday are the law-makers of to-day. . . Just as the democratic cry of a ‘white man’s government,’ created the antagonism between the Irishman and the negro, which culminated in the New York riots of ‘63, so the republican cry of ‘manhood suffrage’ creates an antagonism between black men and all women, that will culminate in fearful outrages on womanhood, especially in the Southern States.” 

>-Source: Elizabeth Cady Stanton, “Women and Black Men,” *Revolution,* 1869

**Stanton’s main purpose in the excerpt was to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: convince the government to give white women the right to vote.
shut down the ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment.
create voting restrictions for African American and immigrant men.
recruit black men to run for office in the federal government

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e3280fe01450ed5eb7d2d69a079be335.jpg)

>-Source: Currier and Ives, [Wikimedia Commons,](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:First_Colored_Senator_and_Representatives.jpg) 1872

**The image most closely reflects which of the following developments in the political climate in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: an increase in voting power for African Americans across the country
an introduction of racial quotas for African American representation in the South
a swell in public outrage over the lack of voting power for white women
a push for literacy tests and poll taxes to determine voter access

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e3280fe01450ed5eb7d2d69a079be335.jpg)

>-Source: Currier and Ives, [Wikimedia Commons,](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:First_Colored_Senator_and_Representatives.jpg) 1872

**Which of the following most directly led to the circumstances illustrated by the image?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the ratification of an amendment extending suffrage to African American men
the creation of a political party aimed at filling federal positions with African American men
the introduction of Black Codes in the South to protect African American voting power
the establishment of the Freedmen’s Bureau to enforce federal voting laws in the South

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e3280fe01450ed5eb7d2d69a079be335.jpg)

>-Source: Currier and Ives, [Wikimedia Commons,](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:First_Colored_Senator_and_Representatives.jpg) 1872

**The image most strongly supports which of the following arguments about Reconstruction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It opened up political opportunities to formerly enslaved African Americans.
It punished the South for its rebellion against the federal government.
It did not effect any real change for African Americans in the South.
It revolutionized life in the northern states, but not the southern states.
